sentences.text: Yes.
Uh, uh, I think it's fair to say I was impressed by the paramilitarism of the Hitler Youth.
We lived, before we moved into my grandfather's house, in a flat that was not too far away from an old, uh, uh military barracks that was continued to be used by the German army.
And very often, that parade, that was a, a cavalry unit.
And they paraded around on horseback.
And they had a terrific band.
And I was always impressed by the, by the, the big drummer who had these kettle drums on either side of the horse, and the way he was waving around with his, uh, drum sticks, as they were.
Uh, the military music impressed me.
The precision impressed me.
And of course, that was translated later on into the Hitler youth.
They marched around in hordes and in groups and columns and, uh, in, uh, military units.
And I felt sad to some considerable extent that I was not in the position to join them.
I would have been quite happy in, under such circumstances.
The precision, the discipline, the military, um, splendor, you know [LAUGHS], italics, if I can call it that.
That, Of course, applies to all military units, not only of the Nazis.
But I mean, th-- that was the only thing I had ever seen.
And yes, I, I often felt what a pity that I couldn't join this.
And there was, of course, nothing equivalent amongst the Jewish youth for this type of thing.

There was an organization similar to Ajax in this country to which my father belonged as well, which was the Association of Jewish Front Line Soldiers, I think would be the correct translation, the Reichsbund judischer Frontsoldaten, as it was called in German.
But they didn't parade.
They didn't parade.
And they wanted to keep themselves as far away from any paramilitarism or any military discipline that the Nazis were so fond of, a-a-as, as, as possible.
So yes, I, I, I, I had this feeling as a young boy, what a pity I couldn't get into this.

And had I grown up a normal life here in England, let us assume if I would have been born here in England, then I dare say I would have joined the Boy Scouts and then later on most probably the Cadet Force.
That was, you know [LAUGHS], a sort of driving force, as it were.
So there was a conflict about being a fairly studious though active Jewish boy?
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
That was always impressive.

And I daresay, if you see some of the pictures of, uh, the way they paraded with their flags and with their symbols and so on, I, I, I'm impressed by this, in the same way as I'm impressed by the, uh, the, the, the trooping of the colors, for instance, here in London.
This is an impressive military display I'm, I'm very fond of.
Yes, I suppose so.
In the back of my mind, I knew these people.
And some of them were people with whom I went to school in the earlier stages were amongst those who were beating me up.

But, uh, since I never suffered a broken leg or often came home with a bleeding nose or perhaps a scratch or two on my legs or so, I didn't, it didn't demure me all that much from retaining that little bit of respect for that sort of pompous militarism.
[SIGHS] Maybe I did.
I don't remember that.
And if I did, I suppose my parents [LAUGHS] would have tried very much to try and, uh, sort of, uh, prevent me from developing any, any further such views.
Well, I think it's possibly inherent in all young boys.
And if you look at the young people today, they, they play with toy guns.
And they play the hide and seek and coppers and, and, and thieves and red Indians and cowboys and so on.
I think this aggressiveness, perhaps, is inherent in all the male children.
And, uh, it was no different with us, I suppose.
Yes.
Yes.

As I said earlier, that Goring-- and he had a very good reason for this-- Goring, in fact, decreed that all those who had been with the Imperial German forces in the first World War should be released.
That also included my father and raised our hopes.
Now, Buchenwald is a long distance away from Breslau.

And because the inmates were all shaven and were in a very bedraggled sort of appearance, to say the very least, the Germans very cleverly transported them or let them go home by train only during the night so that the smallest possible number of German citizens would ever come across them.

And I do remember that the train, the only train, that would, in fact, arrive in Breslau from Buchenwald-- I don't know that whether it was a direct train or whether one had to change somewhere-- would arrive in Breslau early in the morning at about 6:30.
And it was winter, of course.
There was plenty of snow on the ground.
My grandfather, my father's father, got up every morning early in the morning and walked around in the garden in the snow so that he could greet his son when, in fact, he came home.
And one morning, he overslept.
And it was that morning when my father actually came home.
He took a taxi or whatever he did from the station.
I don't know.
And he knocked at the door.
My mother was there to open it up.
And, uh, he was home.
My grandfather was very upset that he had missed him.
But, uh, what happened after that was fairly traumatic, certainly for me.
And my younger brother, Walter, perhaps didn't quite appreciate the, the, the, the situation in so far that for three weeks, we were not allowed to even say hello to our father.
He was in his bedroom.
And the house had to be very, very quiet.
He was not well, we were being told by my mother.
And however much we pressed and wanted to see him, th-- th-- that was not permitted.
And in retrospect, perhaps rightly so.
He must have looked so haggard, so terrible that it may well have been a very severe shock to both of us.
I was then 12.
My younger brother was eight.
It wasn't, perhaps, the sort of scene for young boys.
And after about a week or so after his return, my mother asked me one morning to dig a hole in the garden.
And that was a terrible shock.
When I asked her what was that for, fearing the worst, she said, just please dig it.
And I'll explain that later on.
And so I dug.
I remember, it was a tough task because the ground was frozen.
The snow had to be cleared away first of all, and then the ground in itself was frozen.
But [LAUGHS] the hole that I dug at the age of 12 was much for me not a very, very sort of respectable one.
Then I saw my mother, uh, carrying a bundle of clothes.
And I assumed that those must have been the clothes that my father wore when he came home from the concentration camp.
And she buried that.
And she, herself, in fact, leveled out-- in other words, shoveled the, the, the soil back on top of the clothes.
And to a very large extent, I was greatly relieved.

Because I, um, sort of had all sorts of dreadful thoughts that maybe I was digging a grave here for my own father without, in fact, ever having seen him again after I last saw him before he went to Buchenwald.
Well, slowly but surely, he recuperated.
He lost a number of toes.
He had serious lacerations on his back due to inhumane punishment that, in fact, he had to undergo in, in, in, in the concentration camp.
They were asked to stand in rows to attention for three or four hours in the freezing cold and the biting winds.
He experienced his 50th birthday while he was in the concentration camp.
He was born on the 25th of November.
And his colleagues in the hut in which he was housed collected, found an old, rusty tin, so he told me.
That they collected some rain water off the roof or so and presented that to him.
This was an extra ration of water.
And they also presented him with two or three torn sheets of newspaper, which were in absolute luxury in the- in the camp.
People were, apart from incredibly cruel treatment, were kept to an absolute minimum.
Food was very, very bare.
Drink was very, very bare.
And necessities such as toilet paper were not available, so, so-- on purpose, of course.
So people had to make, mend, and do the best possible way they could.
It seems to me that, uh, the whole treatment of these people was not only of a physical nature but also, of course, very much of a psychological nature.
Oh, no.
Uh, these are, in fact, uh, reports that I got from various other people who had been to concentration camps.
I never got this-- the only information I got from my father were these two birthday presents.
He would never tell me anything else about what happened to him.
However much I begged him, especially before he died, uh, he would not reveal any details whatsoever.
So slowly but surely, one had to piece together some of the experiences these poor people had to go through.
And don't forget, not everybody survived.

Uh, I had an uncle who was in the concentration camp and who committed suicide there by running himself into the electrified fence, simply and purely because he just couldn't take it any longer, I suppose.
His son now lives in Australia.
Would you like to tell us his name?
Yes.
My uncle's name was Felix, Dr.
Felix Mendelsson.
And his son lives still in Australia.
He's 81 years old.
And uh, we are, of course, in touch.
And we visit each other.
We, we, I, I saw him last three years ago when my wife and I were in Australia.
Yes.

Well, uh, by, by the time my father was in the concentration camp, m-my mother, who was a very courageous woman and a very assertive lady, literally went to Gestapo headquarters every day to demand her husband's release.

And She tells me, and I think this is very true, although she told it to me, she made such a nuisance of herself in a dignified manner that the Nazis started to gain a considerable amount of respect for her and offered her, for instance, a seat to sit down.
And she was quite fit.
She was in her middle 40s at that time.
Whereas other women who much older still had to stand up and so on.
They developed some kind of a preferential treatment for her.
And, uh, she fought like hell to try and get him out of there.
But, of course, that in itself was of no avail.
She also instigated tremendous attempts to various countries, to various embassies, to try and get her small family out of Germany.

And I think Buchenwald, however horrendous it was, even to our family-- and my father survived it, there were lots of people who didn't survive it-- was perhaps a motivator to do something constructive about getting out of there.

I do remember that at that time h-here in England-- and this is a remarkable story, even by today's standards-- the, uh, an appeal was made to the House of Commons that, uh, one ought to try and save as many children as possible.
And it seems incredible by today's standards that that appeal was made a day or two after the Kristallnacht, let's say on the 10th, 11th, or 12th of November.
There seemed to be a considerable amount of majority voting for this sort of thing, cross party thing.
And do you realize that by December of that same year, just six or seven weeks after the appeal was made in the House of Commons, the first train loads of children actually rolled into this country?
Now, we got to remember that Britain was the only country that did something of this nature.
And the original intention of saving something like 50,000 children, uh, was never reached.
I think they saved something like about 11 or 12,000 children.
And they came over here on this famous Kindertransport.
Yes.
Uh, fortunately, my brother and I were amongst those 10 or 12,000 who, in fact, were saved that way.
So my parents became aware of this, and of course, signed onto this straightaway.
And we were fortunate enough, with a little bit of help, perhaps financial help, or guarantee as it were, a financial guarantee from an uncle of mine who had married my mother's sister.
And this uncle was an English-born Jew, had, in fact, uh, guaranteed financial security so that we would not be a burden to the state.
But we were integrated into this Kindertransport movement.
The Kindertransport itself, I, I, I don't know.
W-we were notified when we'd have to leave.
There were very, very strict rules and regulations on this.
The Nazis raised no objections, provided we had not any specific debts to the state, but as children, obviously, who were not due to income tax or anything of this nature.
So from that point of view, I should imagine there was no problem there.
But they stipulated that we could only take as much as we could carry ourselves.
Now, you can imagine that 12 and eight-year-old boy was not, uh, going to carry a tremendous amount of equipment.
And by equipment, I mean basically clothes.
I remember, for instance, that, uh, my father and I think my mother sat on the suitcase in order to close the thing.
It was so chockablock full of clothes.
And the suitcase of my brother was, of course, somewhat smaller than mine.
Because the bastard Nazis at the station at Breslau-- it was all by train-- in fact, demanded that we showed to them that we were able of carrying that suitcase.
[LAUGHS] You know, we dragged this thing along the platform.
And we passed the test, I suppose.
And so we were off into the train.
Now, the point to remember here is that my case was exactly like everybody else's.
Parents kissed their children goodbye without knowing ever if they were going to see them again.
My case was somewhat different, which perhaps I can elaborate on a little later.
But, uh, the sacrifice that these parents made must have been tremendous.
At that time, obviously, to me, this was all terrific excitement-- a new country, a train journey.
I had never been on the train for quite such a distance.
Breslau is in eastern Germany, as I mentioned before.
So this went all the way across Germany and all the way across Holland to Hoek van Holland.
Then, we were going to go on a boat-- I'd never been on a boat before-- the ferry from Hoek van Holland to Harwich, and then again into a train.
So this was terrific excitement.
Oh, yes, very much so, very much so.
It was an adventure, as far as I was concerned.
Of course, I realized I wouldn't be able to see the parents for a week or so.

But you know, the-- if you put these things on balance as a young boy, or as a young child perhaps, uh, this excitement, this terrific adventure, took over and, and suppressed, perhaps, any of the other emotional feelings they must have felt.
It must have been very much different to my parents and to all the other parents.
And having been a parent now myself for many years, [LAUGHS] beginning to realize what a fantastic sacrifice that must have been.
But the only a satisfaction that these parents must have felt, that their children were going to be safe, somehow, somewhere, God knows how.
Nobody knew who was going to take care of them.
They were going to go to England, which was considered to be a Garden of Eden.
They were going to be safe.
Now.
Yeah.
With regards to my brother?
No.
No, the, the usual sort of things-- always be a good boy.
Always be honest, you know.
Never, never forget that you're Jewish.
Never forget your parents.
Never forget your family, and so on and so forth.
But basically, all that-- I'm sure they must have done.
But you know, that landed on deaf ears because the excitement and everything else was quite incredible.
I remember the biggest difficulty was to move around.
We were all growing children.
And nobody knew, of course, who was going to provide the next shirt or the next pair of trousers.
And so I wore two shirts, three sets of underwear.
Oh, yes, yes, yes.
I had a jacket and two overcoats.
And they were all, each and every one, was a bigger size.
So you know, we all looked like this.
And we could hardly, hardly move around, in order to at least provide for the next six or 12 or 18 months of clothes.
And nobody knew who was going to take care of us.
Were we going to go into a camp?
Were we going to go into a family?
Were we going to be in a hostel, whatever happened?
The main thing was out of Germany and into safety.
On our transport, possibly from Breslau, there may have been about 20.
We then spent the night in Berlin, where other transports of that nature were being organized.
And I suppose on our trip, ultimately in England, there must have been about 150, 160 who arrived there.
Um, not as far as I know, no, no, no.
Certainly not those outside Breslau, and from Breslau itself, I don't recall anybody, no, no.
And perhaps one of the sad things was to leave one's own friends behind, who perhaps matter at the moment more than one's parents.
Peers have a tremendous influence on people, especially when they're young.
Up to the age of 30, 35 or so, I think, that- that- that plays a very important part, and certainly played an important part to us.
I was very unfortunate.
As we came to the Dutch border, the German passport control came in, the customs people.
And they were, of course, all Nazis.
[LAUGHS] And they didn't look at every suitcase, but they certainly picked-- and we were about eight or 10 in the compartment in the group-- th-- they picked my brother's suitcase.
And I had to get this out of the luggage rack.
And I had to open it up.
When I, as I released the lock, of course, the, the, the lid spraying out.
And th-- th-- this bloody German dug his hands into there and threw everything out of there.
I don't know what he was suspecting we were smuggling or what.
He found nothing.
He said, good.
And now, of course, the problem was how the hell can I pack all this into there again?
So my brother and I stuffed underwear and socks into coat pockets, on top of everything else.
We were basically like scarecrows walking around, you know, like this, because we, we could hardly sort of keep our arms to one side.
Th-there was too much sort of textile material between us.
We must have looked like scarecrows.
But somehow, we managed to get across here.
In Holland, of course, there was a wonderful reception by relief organizations, Jewish and non-Jewish.
And then when we got to [INAUDIBLE], there was a similar sort of a reception again by totally interdenominational people who meant well, who were obviously caring, and so on and so forth.
And ultimately, we got to London.
And in London, we were met-- that's my brother and I, we were extremely fortunate-- by my aunt and uncle.
Middlebrook, Sam and Lenny Middlebrook, who lived in London.
And they took us to their home.
And ultimately, they felt it would be better if we were with other children.
They had no children of their own.
And so we were sent to a hostel for refugee children that was run-- this particular one-- was run by B'nai B'rith and was, in fact, the Margate in Kent, right.
Yeah, right by the sea.

What I think might be of interest to you, and I'd like to come back again to the concentration camp period, if I may-- the reason why, in fact, Goring suddenly decided that he wanted to, uh, release all those who fought in the First World War was that when he himself was a fighter pilot with the Luftwaffe in the First World War.
And I think he had about 18 kills to his credit.
He was in serious difficulty by a French pilot who started following him and shooting at him, and then certainly damaged his aircraft.
And one of Goring's squadron colleagues, another Luftwaffe pilot, managed to eliminate the Frenchmen so that Goring could sort of crash land, as it were, in peace and quiet.
You've got to remember, in those days, aircraft were flying at fantastic speeds of about 60 or 65 miles per hour.
They weren't at a fantastic height.
The impact, of course, was considerable.
But nevertheless, the planes usually fell into bits and pieces.
And if you were really lucky, you managed to crawl out of the debris, which Goring did.
Now, he quite clearly was very grateful to his squadron mate for having saved his life.
And it so happened that he was Jewish.
And so Goring literally owed his life to this Jewish colleague of his.

This Jewish colleague, who oddly enough, also lived in Breslau and was an acquaintance of my grandfather, was approached by Goring in 1934 or '35 and offered a very senior post in the German Luftwaffe.
And although he was Jewish, he accepted this with great honor and was perhaps the only Jew who served in the Luftwaffe.
He had the rank of an air marshal, General der Flieger, as the Germans call the rank.
And he was in charge of, uh, uh, provisions, ammunition, logistics, and so on and so forth.
Yeah.
Out of gratitude by Goring, the Nazi to the Jew who saved his life, he felt that perhaps he ought to do something for those who were also in the German forces in the first World War.
And so.
Yes.
OK.
